perturbation.original_text: Payment of the Invoice Total is due (without any right of set-off) Net forty-five (45) days after the date of invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment of the Invoice Total is due (without any right of set-off) Net ninety (90) days after the date of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 90 days could violate prompt payment laws if the Airspan Products are goods, and if South Dakota law (where the Distributor's office is located) requires payment within a shorter timeframe for certain goods or services contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law states the interest rate on judgments and decrees, and contracts. By extending the payment period without consent, this law may be violated if there is no agreement to such terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Loading... | South Dakota Legislature We're sorry but South Dakota Legislature Website doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue. Your browser is not supported Please update your browser or download one of the following browsers: Chrome Firefox Edge
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the payment period from 45 to 90 days, potentially conflicting with prompt payment laws in South Dakota if the Airspan Products are goods that require a shorter payment timeframe. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this potential violation. The Law Explanation suggests that extending the payment period without agreement could violate laws related to interest on contracts. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition states a contradiction occurs when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the terms unenforceable. The scraped snippet doesn't load, but the explanations provided indicate a potential contradiction based on prompt payment laws in South Dakota.

perturbation.original_text: If Distributor disputes any invoice or part thereof, Distributor must notify Airspan in writing within twenty-five (25) days of the invoice date giving details of the reason for such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: If Distributor disputes any invoice or part thereof, Distributor must notify Airspan in writing within five (5) days of the invoice date giving details of the reason for such dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the dispute notification period to 5 days may be considered unreasonably short, particularly if the Distributor is a small business. This could run afoul of general contract law principles requiring fair and reasonable terms, especially in jurisdictions like South Dakota, where unconscionable contract terms are disfavored.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute prohibits unconscionable contracts. A 5-day dispute window may be deemed unconscionable if it's unreasonably short and oppressive to the Distributor.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Loading... | South Dakota Legislature We're sorry but South Dakota Legislature Website doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue. Your browser is not supported Please update your browser or download one of the following browsers: Chrome Firefox Edge
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time for dispute notification from 25 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this might be unreasonably short and potentially unconscionable, especially for small businesses, and cites South Dakota as a jurisdiction where unconscionable contract terms are disfavored. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a 5-day dispute window could be deemed unconscionable if unreasonably short and oppressive. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, the context strongly suggests that South Dakota law addresses unconscionable contracts, supporting the argument that the reduced timeframe contradicts legal principles of fairness, which aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For at least two (2) years after termination of this Agreement, Distributor will maintain its records, contracts, and accounts relating to distribution of Airspan Products, and will permit examination thereof by authorized representatives of Airspan at all reasonable times.
perturbation.changed_text: For at least six (6) months after termination of this Agreement, Distributor will maintain its records, contracts, and accounts relating to distribution of Airspan Products, and will permit examination thereof by authorized representatives of Airspan at all reasonable times.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping period to 6 months after termination could violate statutes of limitations for breach of contract or fraud claims. Given that this contract applies to South Dakota, the statute of limitations is a relevant factor.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law states that the statue of limitations for contract actions in South Dakota is six years. Shortening the record retention to only 6 months makes it more difficult, and in some cases impossible, for claims to be filed under this statue.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a two-year record retention period post-termination, while the modified version reduces this to six months. The Law Explanation indicates that the statute of limitations for contract actions in South Dakota is six years. Reducing the record retention period to six months could significantly hinder the ability to pursue legal claims within the six-year statute of limitations, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined under Inconsistencies.
